chunk_summary: Guidelines for managing cow's milk protein allergy (CMPA) in infants and young children, including breastfeeding strategies, formula selection, complementary feeding, and reintroduction protocols.
learning_objectives: Understand the clinical presentation and diagnosis of cow's milk protein allergy (CMPA) in infants.
Learn appropriate feeding plans, including maternal dietary elimination and formula selection for CMPA management.
Identify strategies for nutrient compensation during complementary feeding in CMPA cases.
Explore reintroduction protocols for cow's milk in children who may outgrow CMPA.
key_facts: CMPA can present with symptoms like eczema, vomiting, diarrhea, and blood-streaked stools in the first year of life.
Maternal elimination of dairy is the first-line strategy for exclusively breastfed infants with suspected CMPA, with clinical improvement expected in 1–2 weeks.
Extensively hydrolyzed formula (EHF) is recommended for supplementation; amino acid formula (AAF) is reserved for severe cases or non-response to EHF.
Soy formula can be considered after 6 months, but goat or ruminant milks should be avoided due to cross-reactivity.
Complementary feeding should exclude milk-containing foods, with nutrient compensation via iron-rich foods, calcium-fortified products, and supplements.
reference_quote: Diagnosis and assessment of food allergy — National Institute for Health and Care Excellence — https://www.nice.org.uk/guidance/cg116
